1:
The chapter opens on a poor widow's cry, and the witnesses read it first as a scene where law and love collide. The grammatical-historical commentators set the legal stage. Keil and Delitzsch explain that the Mosaic law gave a creditor the right to claim the person and children of an insolvent debtor, who then served as bondmen until the year of jubilee set them free (Lev. 25:39–40); Charles Ellicott adds that the older Roman law was harsher still, providing no such future release. Ellicott also presses a point of social history: these wives of 'the sons of the prophets' prove the prophetic guilds were no celibate, monastic order — heads of families kept their own separate homes (cf. 1 Kings 20:35). John Wesley and John Gill agree that the prophets married as freely as priests and Levites, for prophecy, as Matthew Henry notes, 'went not by entail, as the priesthood did.'

A lively debate surrounds the dead man's identity. The Targum, Josephus, and the Jewish writers, with Ephrem Syrus, identify him with Obadiah, Ahab's God-fearing steward who hid the prophets by fifty in a cave (1 Kings 18:3–4), supposing he had spent all his goods maintaining them. Gill and Ellicott are frankly skeptical; Gill judges her 'more likely the wife of a meaner person,' and Ellicott files the tradition away with a wry mark of doubt. Adam Clarke, following Jarchi, instead names the creditor as Jehoram son of Ahab, who had lent Obadiah money at usury. But the widow's own plea settles the theology: she grounds her appeal on the fact that 'thy servant did fear the Lord.' Wesley draws the moral edge — his poverty 'was not procured by his idleness, or prodigality, but by his piety,' for he would not comply with the king's worship and so lost every worldly advantage; he was, Henry says, one of the seven thousand who had not bowed the knee to Baal.

Clarke turns the creditor's severity into a long survey of the ancient custom of selling children for debt among Romans, Athenians, and Asiatics, closing with his own protest against the 'senseless and pernicious custom' of the debtor's prison; Isaiah had already alluded to the practice (Isa. 50:1), and our Lord to it in the parable of the unforgiving servant (Matt. 18:25). Against this dark backdrop Henry sounds the pastoral note the witnesses land on together: a godly minister may still die poor and in debt through affliction or persecution — 'the children of light are not always wise for this world' — yet the widow comes to Elisha 'in dependence upon the promise that the seed of the righteous shall not be forsaken.' Because her husband was known to the prophet as a man who feared God, she rightly looks to the one who is a father to the sons of the prophets and their families. Elisha's miracles, Henry insists, were 'for use, not for show' — acts of real charity, like the works of Christ, in which God magnifies his goodness with his power.
2:
Elisha's opening question — 'What shall I do for thee? ... what hast thou in the house?' — is weighed differently by the witnesses. John Gill hears in it the prophet's own poverty: unable to relieve her out of his substance and knowing nowhere to turn, he can only pity her, give his best advice, and pray. Matthew Henry reads it more constructively: Elisha 'considered her case' and inquired what she had to make money of, for though the sons of the prophets were too poor to relieve her by a collection, 'the God of the holy prophets is able to supply all her need,' and if she has but a little committed to her management, that little will be multiplied by his blessing. Had she owned any plate or furniture, Henry adds, he would have bidden her sell it first, to be just to her creditors; but the divine power will work upon what is actually in the house.

The one asset is a solitary pot of oil, and the grammatical-historical witnesses linger over it. Adam Clarke observes that oil served for food, for anointing the body after bathing, and for anointing the dead, and that some suppose this flask was the very oil she had kept back for her own burial (comparing the woman who anointed Christ, Matt. 26:12). Charles Ellicott presses the philology: the rare word 'âsûk denotes not a mere flask but 'oil enough for an anointing,' which the Vulgate renders 'a little oil, wherewith I may anoint myself' (cf. Micah 6:15; and the custom of anointing after the bath, 2 Sam. 12:20). He notes, too, that the peculiar form of the pronoun here points to the northern origin of the narrative rather than to a late composition.

Beneath the exegetical detail the witnesses agree on the lesson. Grace begins with the little already in our hand: the fountain of supply is not conjured out of nothing but poured through the one vessel she possesses. As Henry puts it, God can teach us 'to make the best of what we have,' for the way to increase what we have is to use it under his blessing. The widow's empty house holds one small flask — and that is enough for God to begin.
3:
The prophet's next word turns the widow outward to her neighbours: 'Go, borrow thee vessels abroad of all thy neighbours, even empty vessels; borrow not a few.' John Gill notes the plain implication that Elisha 'perceived she had none' of her own left; Matthew Henry fills in the picture, judging that she had already sold her own vessels toward satisfying her creditors, and that the prophet, 'knowing her to have credit among her neighbours,' bids her draw on that goodwill. Charles Ellicott sharpens the two key phrases: 'abroad' means literally from the outside of the house, out of doors, and 'borrow not a few' is an urging not to 'scant or stint' herself in the borrowing.

That last stress becomes the heart of the verse for the witnesses. The command is deliberately open-ended: the measure of the miracle will be set not by the oil but by the number of empty vessels she is willing to gather. Henry draws the point out — 'were there more vessels, there is enough in God to fill them; enough for all, enough for each' — so that whatever limit the supply meets will be a limit of her own faith and diligence, not of God's bounty. The instruction to borrow widely, and from every neighbour, is thus itself a summons to expect much; a timid, stinting request would receive a timid, stinting answer.

There is also a quiet dignity in the arrangement. The oil will not fall as manna from heaven but will flow through the ordinary channels of neighbourly kindness and the widow's own errand; she must go, ask, and carry. The empty vessels she collects, standing ready and open, become the classic emblem the later expositors will seize upon at v. 6 — that God fills only what is brought empty to him, and fills it to the very brim of its capacity. There is faith in the very asking, too, for she must let her neighbours see how bare her cupboards have become and trust that the shame of begging so many empty jars will be swallowed up in the wonder of what fills them. The prophet's charge to 'borrow not a few' is, in the end, an invitation to enlarge her expectation of what God is about to do.
4:
The instruction grows more particular: 'when thou art come in, thou shalt shut the door upon thee and upon thy sons, and shalt pour out into all those vessels, and thou shalt set aside that which is full.' John Gill marks only the plain sense of coming in 'into her house,' but the fuller comment belongs to the shut door. Charles Ellicott offers two reasons for it: to avoid disturbance from without, and perhaps because publicity was undesirable in the working of such a miracle — comparing our Lord's habit of enjoining secrecy on those he healed and of excluding the crowd (Luke 8:51; 8:54). The setting-aside of the full vessels, he adds, is to be done 'by the help of thy sons,' who thus share in the wonder.

Matthew Henry gathers these threads into a practical devotion. She must shut the door, he says, to prevent interruption from the creditors and others while the work was in hand, so that they might not 'seem proudly to boast of this miraculous supply,' and so that the family might have 'opportunity for prayer and praise to God upon this extraordinary occasion.' He notices, too, that the oil is to be poured out 'by herself, not by Elisha nor by any of the sons of the prophets' — an intimation that it is in connexion with our own careful and diligent endeavours that we may expect the blessing of God, for 'what we have will increase best in our own hand.'

So the closed door frames the miracle as a private transaction of faith. Away from the pressure of the creditor and the eyes of the curious, the widow and her two sons are shut in alone with the promise; the household that had faced bondage together now labours together in hope. Faith, the commentators imply, often does its truest work unseen, in the sealed room where only God and the believing heart are present, far from the crowd that would either mock or applaud. The secrecy is not superstition but reverence — the same instinct that would later shut the door upon the dead child at v. 33, keeping the holy work of God from the noise and applause of the world, and reserving it for prayer.
5:
The narrative now shows the widow doing exactly as she was told: 'So she went from him, and shut the door upon her and upon her sons, who brought the vessels to her; and she poured.' John Gill underlines the temper in which she acted — she 'did as he advised her, borrowed many empty vessels of her neighbours, having faith in what the prophet had said to her.' The whole obedience turns on that faith; as Matthew Henry observes at large, she did not object that the prophet 'designed to make a fool of her,' but 'firmly believing the divine power and goodness, and in pure obedience to the prophet,' she poured out her one flask into vessel after vessel.

Charles Ellicott attends to the Hebrew, and finds the verse pulsing with life even in its grammar. The form rendered 'from him' is the correct classical one; and the clause about the sons should be pointed with a break, so that the sense runs on: 'they were bringing to her, and she was pouring continually.' The verb for her pouring, he notes, occurs only here, and paints an unbroken action — 'she did not leave her pouring.' The story, he adds, 'is evidently abridged in this verse,' compressing what must have been a long and breathless labour into a single line.

Read together, the witnesses see obedient faith made visible in ceaseless motion. One son runs to fetch the empty vessels, the other carries away the full; the mother stands at the centre pouring, and pouring, and pouring. There is no hesitation, no testing of the promise, no pause to marvel while the work is undone — only the quiet, strenuous trust that keeps the oil moving from hand to hand behind the shut door. The witnesses notice, too, that the whole household is drawn into the labour: the mother pours while her two sons — the very children the creditor had come to seize — run to and fro with the vessels, so that the family threatened with bondage now works together in the freedom of faith. The verse is a small portrait of what Henry calls believing obedience: taking God at his word and acting upon it to the full, before the wonder is complete or the reason understood.
6:
The climax comes when the vessels give out before the oil does: 'Bring me yet a vessel. And he said, There is not a vessel more. And the oil stayed.' John Gill records the wonder plainly — the oil 'being miraculously increased as it was poured forth' filled every vessel — and preserves the curious rabbinic conceit of Ben Gersom and Abarbanel that 'when the pot was emptied, all the air that entered it was turned into oil.' Charles Ellicott works at the words: 'her son' is probably the eldest (the LXX has the plural), and 'stayed' means literally 'stood,' that is, halted or stopped (as the issue of blood 'stood still' at Luke 8:44); he brushes aside Bähr's reading that the verb means the oil 'continued to flow.'

It is Adam Clarke who lifts the moment into its great emblem. 'While there was a vessel to fill, there was oil sufficient; and it only ceased to flow when there was no vessel to receive it. This is a good emblem of the grace of God. While there is an empty, longing heart, there is a continual overflowing fountain of salvation.' And where the oil stops, he says, the fault is never in the fountain: 'if we find at any time that the oil ceases to flow, it is because there are no empty vessels there, no souls hungering and thirsting for righteousness.' John Wesley and Matthew Henry voice the same conviction from the other side: 'we are never straitened in God, in his power and bounty, and the riches of his grace; all our straitness is in ourselves. It is our faith that fails, not his promise.'

Two further notes round out the meditation. The oil stopped, Henry observes, precisely because 'it was not fit that this precious liquor should run over, and be as water spilt on the ground, which cannot be gathered up again' — grace is not wasted, but measured to the vessels prepared. And the whole scene, the witnesses agree, indicts our small expectations more than it magnifies the miracle: 'were there more vessels, there is enough in God to fill them — enough for all, enough for each.' The oil ran to the very limit of her faith and no further, and that limit was hers to set.
7:
The miracle ends in sober counsel: 'Go, sell the oil, and pay thy debt, and live thou and thy children of the rest.' John Gill notes simply that she came and told the man of God what had been done; the weight of the verse lies in the threefold direction that follows. Adam Clarke is struck that Elisha 'does not inveigh against the cruelty of this creditor, because the law and custom of the country gave him the authority on which he acted'; rather than let a poor honest widow's children be sold, or even a Philistine creditor lose what he had lent to a genuine Israelite, the prophet 'would work a miracle to pay a debt which, in the course of providence, it was out of her power to discharge.'

Charles Ellicott attends to the text: an 'and' has dropped out before 'thy children,' so that the Hebrew reads 'and thou — thy sons — thou mayest live'; the archaic pronoun 'attî he takes for a northern dialect form, and 'of the rest' means 'on what is left over' of the price of the oil. John Wesley draws a lesson from the oil's stopping: it 'stayed' to teach us 'that we should not waste any of his good creatures, and that God would not work miracles unnecessarily'; yet within that economy the bounty is boundless — 'were there more vessels, there is enough in God to fill them, enough for all, enough for each.'

Matthew Henry fixes on the moral order of the command, and here the witnesses land together. Her very first care, now that she has the means, 'must be to discharge that' debt, 'even before she makes any provision for her children,' for 'though her creditors were too rigorous with her, yet they must not therefore lose their debt.' It is 'one of the fundamental laws of our religion that we render to all their due, pay every just debt, give every one his own, though we leave ever so little for ourselves' — and this not merely for fear of being sued but 'for conscience' sake,' since 'those that possess an honest mind cannot with pleasure eat their daily bread, unless it be their own bread.' Only then does she live on the remainder — set up, by the prophet's charity, in an honest livelihood. The rescued family is taught justice in the same breath in which it is given relief.
8:
With the second half of the chapter the witnesses feel a deliberate contrast: from the destitute widow to 'a great woman' of Shunem who 'constrained' the prophet to eat bread. Charles Ellicott thinks the juxtaposition of the poor and the rich woman intentional, and locates the scene geographically — Elisha 'crossed over' the plain of Jezreel, which he must traverse whether coming from Samaria or from Carmel, to reach Shunem on the slope of Little Hermon. Adam Clarke places it in Issachar, south of the Kishon at the foot of Tabor, and Matthew Henry paints the household: a woman who 'kept a good house and was very hospitable,' her husband owning a good estate, 'his heart safely trusting in her' (Prov. 31:11), on a road Elisha often travelled (cf. ch. 2:25).

What made her 'great' is quietly disputed. Ellicott and Henry take it of rank and wealth — a woman 'of high rank, or rich,' as the phrase is used elsewhere (1 Sam. 25:2; 2 Sam. 19:33). But Clarke passes on the ancient versions that read her greatness morally: the Chaldee calls her 'a woman fearing sin,' and the Arabic 'eminent for piety before God' — and this, he says, 'made her truly great.' A separate rabbinic tradition, reported by both Clarke and Ellicott, identifies her with Abishag the Shunammite of David's court (1 Kings 1:3); Ellicott dryly demolishes it — she would by then have been more than two hundred years old.

The witnesses agree on the deeper lesson of the scene. However bad things were in apostate Israel, Henry observes, 'God's prophet finds friends wherever he goes,' and this pious matron pressed her hospitality with such importunity that the modest, retiring Elisha, 'loth to be troublesome,' was at last constrained to dine, and thereafter constantly called there. One further note the commentators sound: how naturally the prophet is folded into the life of the home, coming and going on his circuit until a roadside acquaintance ripens into settled friendship, so that her table becomes a fixed stage on his travels. Her greatness, whether of estate or of soul, is shown chiefly in this — that she used what she had to honour a man of God, discerning the treasure that passed regularly by her door.
9:
The Shunammite's proposal begins in wifely deference: 'And she said unto her husband, Behold now, I perceive that this is an holy man of God, which passeth by us continually.' John Gill marks that she was 'not willing to do any thing without his leave and consent,' and Keil and Delitzsch describe the plan she lays before him — to build a small upper chamber for the holy man and furnish it with bed, table, seat, and lamp, whether a chamber walled with brick or one built upon the wall of the house. Her opening word, 'I perceive,' is the fruit of watchful discernment: from his regular passing she has read his character.

The witnesses dwell on two phrases. Charles Ellicott insists that 'holy' here is 'not a merely ornamental or conventional epithet of the man of God, but denotes the special moral elevation of Elisha' — she has seen something in him that sets him apart. And 'continually,' he explains with Adam Clarke, means not without ceasing but 'at stated intervals, regularly'; Clarke supposes his road lay toward some school of the prophets he habitually attended. John Wesley gathers the motive: he is 'a prophet, and that of eminent holiness, by our kindness to whom we shall procure a blessing to ourselves.'

Taken together, the comments present the Shunammite as a model of spiritual perception and domestic order. She notices what a less attentive host would have missed — the holiness of the guest who keeps passing by — and she acts on it, yet not rashly or independently, but in consultation with her husband. Her discernment is practical, moving at once from perceiving to providing; and her reverence is real, for she names Elisha by the very title, 'holy man of God,' that the narrative itself will honour him with. Her example, the commentators add, rebukes a merely formal religion: she does not wait to be asked, nor calculate the cost, but turns her single observation into generous action, reading the man's holiness and answering it at once with a home. The blessing Wesley speaks of will indeed come, but her care is offered first as homage to the God whose servant this man is.
10:
The plan is spelled out: 'Let us make a little chamber, I pray thee, on the wall; and let us set for him there a bed, and a table, and a stool, and a candlestick.' The witnesses read the architecture and the furniture as a study in thoughtful hospitality. Charles Ellicott corrects the sense to 'a little upper chamber with walls' — a room on the flat roof, walled on each side against the weather (cf. 1 Kings 17:19) — where the prophet 'would be secure from all interruption or intrusion on his privacy.' The four items, he notes, 'are the only essentials in Oriental furnishing'; the 'stool' is properly a chair of state, and the 'candlestick' a lamp-stand. John Gill records the Jewish opinion that it was a solid structure of stone or brick, not of reeds.

Adam Clarke and John Wesley read the woman's motive in the design. She knew, says Clarke, that a prophet 'must have need of more privacy than the general state of her house could afford,' and so provided a place where he could live 'in as much privacy as if he were in his own house' — the bed, table, stool, and candlestick being 'every thing he could need.' Wesley hears her reasoning: on the wall, 'that he may be free from the noise of family business, and enjoy that privacy which I perceive he desireth for his prayers and meditations,' furnished with 'bare necessaries,' since 'he cares not for rich furniture or costly entertainment.'

Matthew Henry completes the portrait: the furnishing was to be 'very plain — no costly hangings, no stands, no couches, no looking-glasses, but a bed, and a table, a stool, and a candlestick, all that was needful for his convenience,' not only for repose but for his reading and writing. The witnesses see in this the genius of true kindness: it studies the real needs of the guest rather than the display of the host. She builds not a showpiece but a cell for prayer, fitted exactly to a man whose life was contemplation — and so honours the prophet by understanding him.
11:
The building bears fruit in a single quiet sentence: 'And it fell on a day, that he came thither, and he turned into the chamber, and lay there.' John Gill reads it as habit already forming — he came 'as he had been wont to do' — and Charles Ellicott adds only the small precisions that the room is the walled upper chamber of the previous verse, and that 'lay there' means he 'lay down to rest.' The verse is brief, but the witnesses find in it the meeting of two graces: a hostess who has prepared a place, and a guest humble enough to use it.

Matthew Henry lingers here on the prophet's lack of pretension. Elisha 'seemed highly pleased with these accommodations, for he turned in and lay there,' and — Henry notices — his servant lodged 'in the same chamber, for he was far from taking state.' So famous a man might have expected finer quarters or a household to wait on him; instead he gratefully accepts the little roof-room, sleeping under the same roof as his attendant. The hospitality offered in faith is received in humility.

Read in its place, the verse marks the settled friendship out of which the whole following miracle will grow. The woman's discernment at v. 9 and her careful provision at v. 10 now find their reward simply in this: the holy man of God is at home in her house. He turns in, lies down, and rests — and that rest, freely given and freely taken, becomes the occasion on which the prophet begins to consider, in the verses that follow, how so kind a service might be recompensed. The commentators also let the moment breathe as a picture of mutual trust — the woman content that her labour has found its object, the prophet content to be at home under a stranger's roof — and they see in this settled ease the very reason God will shortly do so great a thing for the house; where kindness and humility meet and abide, mercy is not far off. Small acts of settled kindness, the witnesses suggest, are the soil in which God's larger mercies are sown.
12:
Elisha now moves to repay the kindness, and does so at one remove: 'he said to Gehazi his servant, Call this Shunammite.' The witnesses note first the entrance of a fateful figure. John Gill describes Gehazi as the attendant who ministered to the prophet and lodged in his chamber, 'perhaps one of the sons of the prophets'; Adam Clarke is more ominous, remarking that 'this is the first time we hear of this very indifferent character.' Charles Ellicott adds that his name, first mentioned here, means 'valley of vision,' perhaps from his native place, and that the phrase 'his servant' is literally 'his young man.'

Both Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch are struck that Elisha, though the woman was standing before him, chose to deal with her through Gehazi rather than address her directly. K&D calls this 'striking'; Ellicott judges it unlikely to be a matter of the prophet's dignity, and guesses instead that Elisha thought 'she would express her wishes more freely to Gehazi than to himself.' John Wesley untangles the somewhat compressed narrative, explaining that the verse records in general what the next verses relate in detail — Gehazi sent to call her with a message, her answer, his observation, and finally her coming.

Beneath the mechanics the witnesses see grateful courtesy at work. The prophet who owes so much to this woman will not let the debt lie; he sets his servant to inquire how her kindness may be returned, admitting even Gehazi to the counsel. There is a gentle irony the commentators feel but do not force — that the man now trusted to carry the prophet's grace to the Shunammite is the same 'indifferent character' whose greed will later betray his master and whose failure will darken the raising of her son. There is a further poignancy the commentators feel: that the gratitude of a generous prophet should be entrusted to so ungenerous a heart, and that the message of blessing should travel through the one man in the story whose own end will be a curse. For now, however, he is simply the messenger of a prophet determined to bless the house that blessed him.
13:
The prophet's offer is generous and worldly: 'Thou hast been careful for us with all this care; what is to be done for thee? wouldest thou be spoken for to the king, or to the captain of the host?' Charles Ellicott renders the opening literally — 'thou hast trembled all this trembling' (cf. Luke 10:41) — and notes that the offer itself measures Elisha's standing: he could evidently procure a favour from the king or from 'the commander-in-chief, who was the most powerful person next the king.' Adam Clarke traces that influence to the prophet's part in the late Moabite war, in which Jehoram had reason to believe Elisha 'was the sole cause of his success,' so that a place at court or a post in the army was his to obtain for a friend.

Her reply — 'I dwell among mine own people' — draws out the witnesses' warmest praise. Ellicott paraphrases: 'I am a mere commoner living quietly in the country; I have nothing to seek from such exalted personages.' Clarke turns it into a rhapsody on contentment: 'How few are there like this woman on the earth! Who would not wish to be recommended to the king's notice, or get a post for a relative in the army?' — and he presses the searching contrast between those who would sacrifice peace, conscience, and God himself 'for money, honors, grandeur, and parade,' and this woman who is 'perfectly satisfied and contented with my lot in life.'

Matthew Henry frames the whole exchange as gratitude answered by contentment. 'Those that receive courtesies should study to return them,' and 'as the liberal devise liberal things, so the grateful devise grateful things'; yet she needs no such good offices, for 'it is a happiness to dwell among our own people, and a greater happiness to be content to do so, to know when we are well off.' Henry adds a sober coda: those who dwell securely among their own must not think their mountain cannot be moved, for years later this same woman would in fact need to be spoken for to the king (ch. 8:3–4). The witnesses land together on her quiet dignity — she declines advancement not from pride but from a heart already full.
14:
The prophet, still seeking how to bless her, presses his servant: 'What then is to be done for her?' And Gehazi answers, 'Verily she hath no child, and her husband is old.' John Gill sets the moment — Elisha asking again once Gehazi had reported her contented reply — and Adam Clarke observes that the woman had by now withdrawn, so that the conversation about her deepest need proceeds in her absence. It is Gehazi, moving 'more freely about the house' (as John Wesley notes) while the prophet kept to his chamber, who has seen what she herself would never ask for.

Charles Ellicott weighs the significance of her childlessness, which in Israel 'was at once a misfortune and a reproach.' He gathers the great company of barren women whose grief and eventual relief fill the Scriptures — Rachel, who counted her reproach taken away (Gen. 30:23); Hannah, provoked and weeping (1 Sam. 1:6–7); Elisabeth, whose reproach among men was removed (Luke 1:25) — set against the promised blessing of a fruitful house (Deut. 7:13–14; Ps. 128:3–4). Against that background her silence is eloquent: she had asked nothing, precisely because the one thing she lacked seemed beyond asking.

The witnesses draw the pastoral point that Gehazi's discernment supplies what her contentment concealed. As Matthew Henry puts it, 'those are the most welcome kindnesses which are most suited to our necessities' — and here the prophet's gratitude, guided by a servant's sharp eye, fastens on the very grief she had borne without complaint. The commentators press, too, the tenderness of the exchange: the prophet will not rest in a general goodwill but searches out the exact place where her life aches, and finds it in the empty cradle. The reader also feels the quiet irony that God should use Gehazi, of all men, to name the mercy about to be given; the servant who will later grasp at gain by fraud here does his one plain good, pointing his master toward the childless woman's hidden sorrow. In this small scene the man who will later be struck with leprosy for his greed is, for once, the instrument of an unmixed blessing.
15:
A short verse marks a change of setting: 'And he said, Call her. And when he had called her, she stood in the door.' John Gill hears behind the summons a divine prompting — 'perhaps finding an impulse on his own mind, from the Spirit of God, that this was the thing to be done for her.' The prophet's gratitude has now become a commission; what began as an inquiry into how he might repay her has ripened, under the Spirit's leading, into a definite word he is about to speak.

Charles Ellicott attends to the small but telling detail of where she stands. At v. 12 she had stood before Gehazi; now 'the Shunammite is summoned into the presence of the prophet himself,' yet she 'took her stand' only in the doorway, for 'modesty, or reverence for Elisha, prevented her from going farther.' The same humility that made her a gracious hostess now makes her a reverent suppliant; she comes near enough to hear, but not so near as to presume.

Keil and Delitzsch, reading ahead, place this moment at the threshold of one of the chapter's great works, in which 'the faith of the pious woman was soon to be put to the test, and to be confirmed by a still more glorious revelation of the omnipotence of the Lord, who works through the medium of His prophets.' The verse itself is a hinge: the contented woman who wanted nothing is called back, and stands waiting at the door, on the edge of receiving the one gift she had never dared to seek. The commentators dwell, finally, on the change of place since she first stood before the servant: the woman who kept her distance from Gehazi is now brought to the master himself, yet even here she will come no nearer than the threshold, and reverence and hope meet in that doorway to hold her there, waiting for the word. The witnesses see in her posture the fitting attitude for such a moment — reverent, expectant, and still, the very bearing of a soul that has learned to wait upon God for what it cannot command.
16:
The promise falls with the cadence of an older annunciation: 'About this season, according to the time of life, thou shalt embrace a son.' The witnesses hear at once the echo of Abraham and Sarah. Adam Clarke notes that 'this promise, and the circumstances of the parties, are not very dissimilar to that relative to the birth of Isaac,' and Keil and Delitzsch make the parallel explicit: 'the same favour was to be granted to the Shunammite as that which Sarah had received in her old age, that she might learn that the God of Abraham still ruled in Israel.' Charles Ellicott works over the phrase 'according to the time of life,' rendering it 'at the reviving time' — next spring, or 'when the year has revolved' — the very idiom used of Sarah (Gen. 18:10).

Her cry, 'Nay, my lord, thou man of God, do not lie unto thine handmaid,' the witnesses read with tenderness and some difference. Ellicott takes it as the recoil of a heart afraid to hope: 'raise no delusive hopes' (cf. Isa. 58:11), and he sets her incredulity beside Sarah's (Gen. 18:12–13). Clarke reports a sharper rabbinic reading: 'Do not mock me by giving me a son that shall soon be removed by death; but let me have one that shall survive me' — a plea that will echo with terrible force when the child dies. Whether she fears an empty promise or a fleeting gift, she dares not let herself believe too quickly.

The witnesses land on the wonder that God should stoop to this quiet household as once to the tents of Abraham. The word is precise — a set season, a definite year — and it is sovereign, given without her asking. The promise, the commentators observe, is doubly gracious in that it comes unsought, laid upon a woman who had asked for nothing and had learned to expect nothing; and its very definiteness — a named season, a fixed year — marks it as no vague benediction but the pledge of a God who keeps precise appointments with His people. Her trembling reluctance to believe only magnifies the grace: the barren wife of Shunem is drawn into the ancient story of promise, and told that the God who revived Sarah's dead womb still works among His people through the mouth of His prophet.
17:
The promise is kept to the day: 'And the woman conceived, and bare a son at that season that Elisha had said unto her, according to the time of life.' John Gill tells it plainly — 'quickly after this she conceived, and by that time the year came round, she was brought to bed of a son.' Charles Ellicott and John Wesley both anchor the fulfilment in its Genesis pattern: 'said' means 'promised,' the phrase 'time of life' is the same used of Sarah, and the whole verse answers to the birth of Isaac at the appointed season (Gen. 21:2; and see Gen. 18:10).

Keil and Delitzsch press the point of the fulfilment against her earlier disbelief: 'however incredible this promise might appear to her, as it had formerly done to Sarah, it was fulfilled at the appointed time.' The exactness matters to the witnesses — not a vague blessing but a child 'at that season,' proving that the word spoken through the prophet was the word of the living God, who numbers the months and keeps His appointments. The trembling 'do not lie' of the previous verse is answered by an infant laid in her arms precisely when he said.

Matthew Henry draws the reward home: 'God built up her house, in reward to her kindness in building the prophet a chamber.' The little walled room she raised for a holy man becomes the occasion of a nursery she never expected; the barren wife may now take up the ancient song, 'Sing, O barren, thou that didst not bear' (Isa. 54:1). The witnesses see the whole scene as a parable of divine generosity: she had sought nothing for herself, and received the one gift her heart had silently wanted, given in the very year and season foretold. The commentators press the exactness once more: it is not merely that a son is born, but that he is born 'at that season' and 'according to the time of life,' the word and the event answering each other to the day, so that the child himself becomes a standing proof that the God of the prophets is faithful and true. What God promises, He performs, and He performs it on time.
18:
The narrative leaps forward to a bright, ordinary morning that will turn to grief: 'when the child was grown, it fell on a day, that he went out to his father to the reapers.' John Gill guesses the boy 'perhaps was six or seven years of age, or more,' and Adam Clarke agrees he was old enough 'to go out to the reapers in the harvest field, converse,' and take his part in the day. Charles Ellicott notes only that the opening formula is the same recurring phrase seen at v. 8. The scene is domestic and happy: the son of promise, now a lad, runs out to join his father among the harvesters.

Matthew Henry sets the emotional stage with characteristic tenderness. After the birth of this son, he observes, 'the prophet was doubly welcome to the good Shunammite,' and the child was 'very dear to the prophet, as the son of his prayers, and very dear to the parents, as the son of their old age.' Henry lets us feel the father's pleasure in the boy's company in the field, where 'his joy of his son was greater than the joy of his harvest' — the very fullness of the moment sharpening the blow about to fall.

The witnesses read the verse, then, as the calm before the storm. The child who came by miracle is no hothouse treasure kept indoors but a real boy of the household, out under the harvest sun with the reapers and his father. Everything is as it should be — the promised son grown, the family's joy complete, the ordinary rhythms of labour and love unbroken. The commentators also feel the deliberate ordering of the story: the son is shown to us happy, healthy, and running to his father in the fields, so that when the stroke falls in the next verse it falls upon a fullness of joy, and the trial of the woman's faith is made as heavy as it can be. And it is precisely into this settled happiness that the sudden stroke of the next verse will come, testing the faith of the woman whose barren house God had so lately filled.
19:
The blow falls in a child's own words: 'And he said unto his father, My head, my head. And he said to a lad, Carry him to his mother.' The witnesses are unanimous in their diagnosis. John Gill, following Abarbanel, ascribes it to 'the sun's beating upon it, being harvest time, and hot weather'; Adam Clarke names it a 'coup de soleil, or sun stroke, which might, in so young a subject, soon occasion death, especially in that hot country'; and Charles Ellicott agrees the boy 'had a sunstroke,' his head 'probably uncovered' in 'the hot season of harvest.' Keil and Delitzsch speak of 'inflammation of the brain produced by a sunstroke.' The 'lad' sent to carry him, Ellicott notes, is 'the young man,' a servant waiting on the boy.

Matthew Henry reads the father's response with sympathy: he 'sent him to his mother's arms, his mother's lap, little suspecting any danger in his indisposition, but hoping he would drop asleep in his mother's bosom and awake well.' The homely hope makes the coming loss more poignant — a father who cannot imagine that a headache in harvest will empty his house by noon.

Henry turns the child's cry into a lesson in prayer. 'Whither should we go with our complaints, but to our heavenly Father?' he asks; 'thither the Spirit of adoption brings believers with all their grievances, teaching them to cry, with groanings that cannot be uttered (Rom. 8:26), My head, my head; my heart, my heart.' The commentators hold the two readings together without strain, letting the fatal cause remain entirely natural — a boy's bare head in the noon of harvest — while the scene is made to teach the soul its refuge, that every pain, carried like this child to a stronger arm, is meant to drive us to the Father of mercies. The witnesses thus hold together the plainest natural explanation — a boy struck down by the sun in the fields — and the deepest spiritual use, that our very pains, carried like this child to a father's lap, become the language in which we appeal to God.
20:
The verse is brief and devastating: 'And when he had taken him, and brought him to his mother, he sat on her knees till noon, and then died.' John Gill notes only that 'the lad led him home, and delivered him to his mother'; Charles Ellicott supplies the quiet, painful chronology — 'brought him in,' that is indoors, and 'till noon,' from which 'we gather that the boy was hurt in the forenoon.' A morning's illness, a few hours on his mother's lap, and the child of promise is gone.

Matthew Henry feels the full weight of it. 'The sickness proved fatal; he slept the sleep of death, was well in the morning and dead by noon: all the mother's care and tenderness could not keep him alive.' He draws the sober truth that even the most cherished child is not exempt from mortality — 'little children lie open to the arrests of sickness and death' — and marks the peculiar bitterness of this loss: 'a child of promise, a child of prayer, and given in love, yet taken away.'

The witnesses let the starkness stand without softening it. The son whose birth had been announced like Isaac's, longed for and never asked, dies on the very knees that had received him. There is no deathbed speech, no lingering, only the flat statement that he sat there till noon and died. Yet the scene is not left in despair; the mother's silence here becomes, in the verses that follow, the ground of one of Scripture's boldest acts of faith. They refuse to hurry past the plain horror of it, and yet they will not read it as defeat, for the same brevity that records the death leaves the story open: the child is laid down not into a grave but upon a prophet's bed, and the mother's next act will answer the noonday darkness with the boldest of hopes. For now, the commentators simply honour the grief — the empty lap, the noonday darkness, the promised child laid still — and let the reader feel how sharp is the trial into which God's own gift has led her.
21:
The mother's response is the marvel of the passage: 'And she went up, and laid him on the bed of the man of God, and shut the door upon him, and went out.' John Gill notes she carried him 'into the little chamber built for the prophet,' and Charles Ellicott reads her intent practically — 'she wished to keep the death secret, and the corpse inviolate, during her intended absence.' But the greater witnesses see in the act a deliberate confession of faith. Adam Clarke reasons that 'she had no doubt heard that Elijah had raised the widow's son of Zarephath to life (1 Kings 17:17–24), and she believed that he who had obtained this gift from God for her could obtain his restoration to life.'

Matthew Henry presses the point furthest. Her laying the boy on the prophet's bed is no funeral arrangement but a wager of hope: 'like a genuine daughter of Abraham's faith, as well as loins, she accounts that God is able to raise him from the dead, for thence at first she received him in a figure (Heb. 11:19).' Having heard of the widow's son of Sarepta and knowing that Elijah's spirit rested on Elisha, 'in this faith she makes no preparation for the burial of her dead child, but for its resurrection.' John Wesley echoes it: she was 'apt to believe he that so soon took away what he had given would restore what he had taken away,' for 'by this faith women received their dead raised to life (Heb. 11:35).'

So the witnesses land together on Henry's exclamation, 'O woman! great is thy faith. He that wrought it would not frustrate it.' The shut door of grief becomes a door of expectation; the prophet's bed, where the man of God had so often lain, is now made an altar of hope. Her faith is neither loud nor argued — it is enacted, in the silent carrying of a dead child up the stairs and the quiet closing of a door upon him, in the settled conviction that the God who gave this son could give him back.
22:
Her faith now becomes decisive action: 'Send me, I pray thee, one of the young men, and one of the asses, that I may run to the man of God, and come again.' John Gill pictures her calling to her husband in the field, and Charles Ellicott attends to the naturalness of the language — one of the young men is needed 'to lead and drive the ass,' the animals are properly 'she-asses,' and the promise 'that I may run' carries a 'striking naturalness,' the confident word of a woman who means to be back soon. Keil and Delitzsch add that she 'rode without stopping' to Elisha at Carmel.

What the witnesses feel most in the verse is her extraordinary self-command. Not a word of the child's death passes her lips; she asks only for a mount and a servant, and speaks of returning as though on an errand. Matthew Henry reads the reason in her wisdom — she went 'not acquainting him with her errand, lest he should not have faith enough to let her go.' Her grief is real, but it is governed; she will not spend it in explanation or debate when every moment counts. The single controlling purpose is to reach the man of God.

There is faith in her very haste. To 'run' to Carmel with a dead son laid on the prophet's bed is to act as one who expects that the running will not be in vain. She does not send for a coffin or summon the mourners; she calls for an ass and a driver and sets her face toward Elisha. The commentators feel how much is contained in her silence: no lament, no reproach, no summons to the mourners, only the calm request for an ass and a driver, as though grief itself had been harnessed to a single purpose; faith, they see, is not the absence of sorrow but its mastery. The witnesses see here the practical shape of the great faith praised in the previous verse: it does not sit down under the loss but rises, saddles, and rides — quietly, resolutely, and fast — toward the only help she believes can meet her.
23:
The husband's puzzled question opens a window onto Israelite piety: 'Wherefore wilt thou go to him to day? it is neither new moon, nor sabbath.' John Gill hears him simply asking her reason, but the witnesses fasten on what the question implies. Adam Clarke infers that though 'the religion of the true God was not the religion of the state,' there were 'multitudes of the people who continued to worship the true God alone, and were in the habit of going, as is here intimated, on new moons and Sabbaths, to consult the prophet.' John Wesley agrees these were 'the usual times in which they resorted to the prophets for instruction,' and Matthew Henry pictures the Shunammite as one who, 'though a great woman, attended on public worship' at such seasons.

Charles Ellicott notes the same implication — that the faithful in the northern kingdom 'were wont to visit prophets on these holy days for the sake of religious instruction and edification' — but records a scholarly demur: 'Thenius suggests a doubt whether the later practice of resorting to the Scribes on these days has not here been transferred by an anachronism to the days of Elisha.' The traditional reading takes the detail as a genuine glimpse of devout life under an apostate crown; Thenius suspects a retrojection from a later age.

Her reply, 'It shall be well,' the witnesses read as faith refusing to be delayed. Ellicott would even 'omit "it shall be,"' the Hebrew being like our blunt 'all right' — 'admitting the truth of what is said, yet persisting in one's purpose'; 'she did not want to be delayed, nor to have her faith shaken by argument.' Clarke praises her prudence in sparing her husband's feelings until she had 'tried the power of the prophet,' and Wesley hears her meaning: 'my going will not be troublesome to him, nor prejudicial to thee or me.' The commentators hear in her brief answer the same self-possession that governs the whole journey: she will not be drawn into explanation nor allow her husband's reasonable question to slow her, for a heart set on the prophet cannot afford the delay of debate. She will neither explain nor argue; she will simply go, holding fast the word she is determined shall prove true.
24:
The verse is all urgency: 'Then she saddled an ass, and said to her servant, Drive, and go forward; slack not thy riding for me, except I bid thee.' John Gill notes the saddling was done 'by her order,' and Charles Ellicott refines it — 'she saddled the ass' means the animal 'which the young man brought, and probably saddled at her bidding'; her charge, literally 'restrain me not from riding,' means 'do not stop, or slacken speed.' He observes that on so long a journey 'a halt for rest might naturally be taken,' which is exactly what she forbids.

Adam Clarke fills in the travelling scene from Eastern custom, where 'a servant to walk along side or drive the ass his master rides,' walking behind to goad the beast and guiding its head with the long pole of the goad. He hears in it an echo of the wise man's complaint of a world turned upside down — 'I have seen servants on horses, and princes walking as servants on the earth' (Eccl. 10:7) — though here the roles are ordinary and the only strangeness is the mother's driving haste.

The witnesses see the whole verse as a portrait of single-minded faith in motion. Every detail bends to speed: the ass saddled at once, the servant set to drive, the standing order to press on without pause. The composure she showed before her husband now becomes relentless forward motion; she will not slacken, will not rest, will not be turned aside, until she stands before the man of God. The commentators feel the pathos beneath the urgency: every hurried order — saddle, drive, do not slacken — is the motion of a mother whose child lies dead behind her, yet who rides as one who believes the ride will not be wasted, so that her haste is not panic but hope in a hurry. Her earlier 'that I may run' (v. 22) is here made good in the dust of the road — grief harnessed and driven, hope leaning forward in the saddle toward Carmel.
25:
She reaches her goal: 'So she went and came unto the man of God to mount Carmel.' John Gill recalls that Carmel was 'where Elijah used to be, and where, perhaps, was a school of the prophets,' reckoning the distance from Shunem, on Bunting's authority, at some sixteen miles. Charles Ellicott deduces that Elisha 'must have dwelt there at least occasionally,' and sees Carmel serving 'as a fixed centre of prophetic teaching for the north, as Gilgal, Beth-el, and Jericho for the south.' The mountain of Elijah's great contest is now a settled haunt of his successor.

Keil and Delitzsch carry the scene forward: 'when the prophet saw her from the opposite,' that is, coming in the distance, and recognised her as the Shunammite, he sent Gehazi to meet her and ask after the welfare of herself, her husband, and her child. Ellicott notes that the word for 'afar off' is the same used of the sons of the prophets who watched Elijah's ascent 'to view' from a distance, and that the Syriac calls her the 'Shulamite.'

The witnesses observe the prophet's attentiveness. Elisha knows this woman by sight from far down the road, and something in her solitary, hastening approach moves him to send his servant running to inquire. There is no hint yet of what has happened — the Lord, as the next verses reveal, has hidden it from him — but the prophet's instinct is one of concern, not indifference. The commentators mark, too, the quiet providence in the meeting: that the prophet should be at Carmel, within reach, on the very day of her need, and should know her while she was yet far off, so that before she can speak a word her coming has already been seen and answered with concern. The mother who has ridden without stopping now comes within sight of the man on whose bed her dead son lies, and the meeting toward which all her haste has strained is about to begin. The prophet's swift, solicitous inquiry is itself a mercy, meeting her grief with care before she has so much as uttered a word.
26:
The prophet's threefold inquiry and her one-word answer form the heart of the verse: 'Is it well with thee? is it well with thy husband? is it well with the child? And she answered, It is well.' Keil and Delitzsch explain that she said 'shalom' precisely 'that she might not be detained by any further discussion,' reserving her grief for the prophet's own ear. Charles Ellicott agrees: 'she said this merely to avoid further explanation; she would open her grief to the prophet's own ear, and to none other' — Gehazi is not the man she has ridden to find.

Yet Adam Clarke hears in the same word a profound confession. 'How strong was her faith in God and submission to his authority! Though the heaviest family affliction that could befall her had now taken place, believing that it was a dispensation of Providence which was in itself neither unwise nor unkind, she said, It is well with me, with my husband, and with my child.' Clarke even draws from her calm assurance a doctrine of comfort — that 'the doctrine of reprobate infants had not disgraced the pure religion of the God of endless compassion,' for she had 'no doubts concerning the welfare of her child, even with respect to another world.'

John Wesley and Matthew Henry land where Clarke does. Wesley paraphrases: 'so it was in some respects, because it was the will of a wise and good God, and therefore best for her'; and he draws the rule, 'when God calls away our dearest relations by death, it becomes us to say, it is well both with us and them.' Henry marvels at the paradox — 'all well, and yet the child dead in the house' — and answers it: 'all is well that God does; all is well with them that are gone, if they are gone to heaven, and all well with us that stay behind, if by the affliction we are furthered in our way thither.' The witnesses thus hold both truths at once: her 'It is well' is a shield against premature explanation, and it is a genuine act of trust, spoken over a dead child by a woman still believing that God's dealings are good.
27:
At the summit her composure breaks into passionate entreaty: 'she caught him by the feet: but Gehazi came near to thrust her away.' Charles Ellicott describes the posture — 'she laid hold of, clasped, his feet,' assuming the attitude of 'an humble and urgent suppliant, and no doubt pouring out a flood of passionate entreaties for help' — while Gehazi, thinking 'her vehemence a trespass upon the dignity of his master' (compare the disciples rebuking those who came to Jesus, Matt. 19:13; John 4:27), moves to push her off. Keil and Delitzsch quote the older interpreters that Gehazi judged it 'immodest importunity to wish to urge the prophet, and as it were to compel him.' John Wesley adds that in clasping his feet 'she intimated, what she durst not presume to express in words, that she desired him to go along with her.'

Elisha overrules his servant: 'Let her alone; for her soul is vexed within her.' Wesley reads his tenderness — 'disturb her not, for this gesture is a sign of some extraordinary grief.' Against Gehazi's zeal for decorum stands the prophet's compassion, which will not thrust away a breaking heart on a point of dignity.

But it is the prophet's next words that the witnesses find most weighty: 'the LORD hath hid it from me, and hath not told me.' The commentators are united that this discloses the true nature of prophetic knowledge. Adam Clarke says that 'in reference to this point he had not now the discernment of spirits,' for that gift and prophecy itself were 'influences which God gave and suspended as his infinite wisdom saw good.' Ellicott agrees that 'supernatural knowledge of every event was not a characteristic of the gift of prophecy,' citing Nathan's similar ignorance (2 Sam. 7:3). And Wesley draws the humbling conclusion the prophet himself intends: what Elisha 'knew or did was not by any virtue inherent in himself, but from God, who revealed to him only what and when he pleased.' The man on whose bed a dead child lies confesses that he is no oracle in his own right, but a servant waiting on the disclosures of God.
28:
Her reproach to the prophet is heavy with grief: 'Did I desire a son of my lord? did I not say, Do not deceive me?' The witnesses agree that her first point is that the child was never her own demand. John Gill stresses that 'it was not at her request she had one'; the prophet 'first told her, and assured her she should have one, without her asking for it,' so that her loss cannot be read as the correction of an 'inordinate, importunate desire.' John Wesley makes the same defence: 'this child was not given to me upon my immoderate desire, for which I might have justly been thus chastised, but was freely promised by thee in God's name, and from his special favour.' She had not, like Rachel or Hannah, cried out for a child; the gift was unsought, and therefore its removal is a mystery, not a punishment.

Adam Clarke hears in her words the recoil of a heart that had feared exactly this: 'I expressed no such wish; I was contented and happy; and when thou didst promise me a son, did I not say, Do not deceive me? Do not mock me with a child which shall grow up to be attractive and engaging, but of whom I shall soon be deprived by death.' Her old plea at v. 16 now returns with terrible point. Wesley completes the thought: she would rather never have received the comfort than lose it so soon — 'I had been much happier if I had never had it, than to lose it so quickly.'

Charles Ellicott notes that only 'the conclusion of her appeal is given,' the rest swallowed by sorrow; grief 'does not allow her to specify the cause directly,' so she 'leaves the prophet to infer' the child's death from her broken questions. He hears her saying, in effect, that it were better 'to have had no son, than to have had one and lost him' — the very reverse, he observes, of the poet's later line that it is better to have loved and lost than never to have loved at all; but where that is 'the fruit of reflection,' hers are 'the spontaneous outflow of a mother's poignant sorrow.' The witnesses land together on the pathos: she does not charge God with injustice, but presses upon the prophet the promise he himself had made, as if to say that a word spoken in God's name cannot be suffered to end in an empty grave. Her very reproach is, at bottom, an appeal to the faithfulness of the God who gave.
29:
Elisha's first response is to dispatch his servant with all speed: 'Gird up thy loins, and take my staff in thine hand... if thou meet any man, salute him not... and lay my staff upon the face of the child.' The witnesses read the command to forgo greetings almost unanimously as a charge of urgency. John Gill and John Wesley take the girded loins and the silence on the road as simply making 'quicker dispatch,' and Charles Ellicott agrees it is an 'injunction of utmost haste,' echoing our Lord's like words to the seventy (Luke 10:4), since 'a short greeting might end in a long halt' among Orientals who 'lose much time in tedious salutations.' Keil and Delitzsch record a second, devotional reading from à Lapide and Theodoret — that Gehazi should avoid all distraction and prepare himself by prayer — while noting his known vanity.

The harder question is the staff. Adam Clarke guesses that Elisha thought the case 'might be one of mere suspended animation or a swoon,' the staff serving 'as a stimulus to excite the animal motions.' Ellicott sees deeper intent: the gesture seems to imply 'that if the mother had had faith this would have sufficed for raising the child' (comparing the mantle at the Jordan, ch. 2:8, and the handkerchiefs from Paul, Acts 19:12).

Here the commentators divide. Keil proposes that Elisha 'foresaw the failure of this expedient, and intended by it to teach the Shunammitess and his followers that the power of working miracles was not magically inherent in himself or in his staff, but only in Jehovah' — a design to lift their minds 'to higher and more spiritual conceptions of the prophetic office.' Ellicott records the theory fairly but demurs: 'But this seems doubtful.' The witnesses thus leave the staff an open question, agreeing only that the command was earnest and the errand urgent, and reserving for the next verses the puzzle of why so direct a means should fail. What is not in doubt is the prophet's compassion: at the first word of the child's death he moves at once, sending his fastest help ahead while he follows.
30:
The mother refuses to be sent home with a proxy: 'As the LORD liveth, and as thy soul liveth, I will not leave thee. And he arose, and followed her.' The witnesses hear in her solemn oath the demand for the prophet's own presence, and they weigh what moved her. Adam Clarke reads plain distrust of the errand: 'the prophet, it seems, had no design to accompany her; he intended to wait for Gehazi's return; but as the woman was well assured the child was dead, she was determined not to return till she brought the prophet with her.' John Wesley agrees she 'had no great confidence in Gehazi, nor was her faith so strong as to think that the prophet could work so great a miracle at this distance.'

Keil and Delitzsch propose a gentler reading. Since she swears so vehemently, the question arises 'whether this urging of the prophet arose from doubt as to the result of Gehazi's mission, or whether it was not rather an involuntary utterance of excessive grief, and of the warmest wish of her maternal heart to see her beloved child recalled to life.' They incline to the latter, and find their evidence in the outcome — for Elisha yields to her, arising and following. Her clinging is thus less a verdict on Gehazi than the cry of a mother who will settle for nothing short of the man of God himself.

Charles Ellicott catches a fine touch of the narrator's art: the writer here 'appropriately substitutes "the mother of the child" for "the Shunammite" or "the woman" in connection with this impassioned utterance.' The title tells the tale — it is precisely as a mother, not as a benefactress or a great woman of Shunem, that she grips the prophet and will not let go. And her insistence prevails: the prophet who had meant to send only his staff now rises and goes in person, drawn by a faith that would not be content with anything less than his presence. The witnesses see in it both the honesty of her doubt and the depth of her love, and God's willingness to meet a grief that clings to him.
31:
The staff, sent ahead in Gehazi's hand, accomplishes nothing: 'Gehazi passed on before them, and laid the staff upon the face of the child; but there was neither voice, nor hearing... The child is not awaked.' Keil and Delitzsch render the failure starkly — 'there was no sound and no attention,' the same phrase used of the dead idols of Baal that gave no answer (1 Kings 18:29) — and Charles Ellicott compares the like idiom elsewhere (Isa. 21:7). 'The lad woke not.' The prophet's staff, laid on the dead face, works no miracle at all.

Why it failed is the great question of the passage, and here the witnesses genuinely divide. John Wesley ventures that the disappointment 'might proceed from hence, that Elisha, having changed his mind and yielded to her importunity to go with her, did alter his course, and not join his fervent prayers with Gehazi's action.' Keil and Delitzsch reject any such explanation, insisting it was 'not because of the woman's want of faith, nor because of the vainglory of Gehazi, but simply to promote in the godly of Israel true faith in the Lord.' Ellicott reports and dismisses the rabbinic charge that Gehazi 'had disobeyed his master's injunction by loitering on the way' — 'this is contradicted by the narrative itself,' for 'he had acted with all despatch,' and the prophet 'says no word of censure.' Ellicott also records Bähr's opinion that Elisha 'himself was at fault in supposing he could transfer the spirit and power of a prophet to his servant,' acting 'in over-haste without a Divine incentive' (comparing Nathan's misstep, 2 Sam. 7:3).

The witnesses do not finally agree on the cause, but they converge on the lesson. Whatever the mechanism, the failure of the staff proves that no relic, gesture, or delegated instrument carries miraculous power in itself; life belongs to God alone, and is given only where he himself acts through faith and prayer. Ellicott, with Keil, points back to this as 'the true explanation.' The empty result on the dead child's face clears the stage for the prophet's own approach in the verses that follow, where prayer, not a staff, will raise the boy — teaching the sons of the prophets, and the grieving mother, where the power really lies.
32:
The prophet arrives to face the plain fact of death: 'when Elisha was come into the house, behold, the child was dead, and laid upon his bed.' John Gill notes that he entered 'the chamber built for him,' and Adam Clarke observes soberly that the prophet 'then saw that the body and spirit of the child were separated' — this is no swoon to be roused by a staff, but genuine death. The boy lies where his mother had laid him in faith, upon the very bed of the man of God.

Keil and Delitzsch carry the scene into the great work that follows, and draw a careful comparison with Elijah. Elisha 'shut the door behind them both — himself and the dead child — and prayed to the Lord'; then he lay upon the boy, mouth to mouth, eyes to eyes, hands to hands, bowing down over him until 'the flesh of the child became warm.' They note that this raising 'does indeed resemble the raising of the dead by Elijah' (1 Kings 17:20; and the bowing posture, 1 Kings 18:42), yet 'differs so obviously in the manner in which it was effected, that we may see at once that Elisha did not possess the double measure of the spirit of Elijah.'

The witnesses feel the fittingness of the setting. The chamber the Shunammite built for a holy man, the bed on which he had so often lain down to rest, now becomes the place of resurrection; the faith that had carried a dead son up those stairs is about to be answered on the very spot where it was enacted. There is no panic in the prophet and no pretence — he shuts himself in alone with the corpse and with God, and the reader is made to wait, with the mother below, on the outcome of that closed and praying room. The witnesses also mark the difference from the failed staff: where the lifeless rod laid on the child's face had drawn no response, the prophet now bows himself over the body in prayer, and the flesh begins to warm. The confrontation with real death only sets in relief the greatness of the mercy about to be shown.
33:
The prophet shuts himself in to pray: 'he went in therefore, and shut the door upon them twain, and prayed unto the LORD.' John Gill explains the closed door — upon 'himself and the dead child' — so that 'nobody might come in and interrupt him in his prayers,' nor see the gestures and postures he was about to use. John Wesley agrees he shut it 'that he might pray to God without distraction, and might more freely use those means which he thought fit.' Charles Ellicott notes that the whole account 'imitates' the raising of the widow's son of Zarephath by Elijah (1 Kings 17:17–24), and that 'them twain' means the prophet and the body.

The shut door, appearing here for the third time in the chapter (after the widow's oil at v. 4 and the mother's laying-down of her son at v. 21), the witnesses read as the mark of a work done in secret before God, free from ostentation and from the world's applause. What the room now contains is not magic but prayer: the prophet's first and decisive act, before ever he touches the child, is to cry to the Lord.

Matthew Henry draws the great contrast that governs the whole scene. Elisha and Elijah alike 'did it by petition, as servants,' whereas Christ 'raised the dead to life as one having authority — Damsel, arise (Mark 5:41); young man, I say unto thee, Arise; Lazarus, come forth (John 11:43) — for he was powerful and faithful as a Son, the Lord of life.' The prophet has no life to give of his own; he can only ask it of the God who kills and makes alive. The commentators dwell, too, on the naked simplicity of the act: no incantation and no display, only a man shut alone with a corpse and a cry to heaven, as if to insist that the whole hope of the dead rests not in ritual but in the living God. So the closed door frames not the prophet's power but his dependence, and teaches that those who would see the dead raised — in body or in soul — must first shut themselves in with God and pray.
34:
The prophet stretches himself upon the dead boy: 'he went up, and lay upon the child, and put his mouth upon his mouth, and his eyes upon his eyes, and his hands upon his hands... and the flesh of the child waxed warm.' John Gill notes the bed stood on a raised place, and that Elisha did 'as Elijah did on the widow's son of Zarephath.' Here the witnesses divide over how much is natural and how much divine. Adam Clarke reads it as means employed alongside prayer: the prophet 'endeavored to convey a portion of his own natural warmth to the body,' and 'probably endeavored, by blowing into the child's mouth, to inflate the lungs and restore respiration.' From this he draws his famous maxim: 'Natural means are in our power; those that are supernatural belong to God. We should always do our own work, and beg of God to do his.'

Charles Ellicott and John Wesley put the stress the other way. Ellicott corrects the verb — 'stretched himself' is better 'bowed himself' (with the LXX, Syriac, and Vulgate; cf. 1 Kings 18:42) — and reads the warming not as transferred body-heat but as a miracle of contact: 'the life of the Divine Spirit which was in Elisha was miraculously imparted by contact to the lifeless body,' recalling the breath of life first breathed into man (Gen. 2:7). Wesley likewise denies mere external heat 'which could not be transmitted by such slight touches,' insisting the warmth came 'from a principle of life, which was already infused into the child, and by degrees enlivened all the parts of his body.'

The witnesses do not resolve the tension so much as hold it. The prophet lays his living frame over the dead one, mouth to mouth and eye to eye and hand to hand, in an act at once tender and priestly — an emblem, several sense, of how the servant of God must throw his whole self into the work of reviving the dead. Whether the first flush of warmth be called natural or supernatural, all agree that its true author is the God of life, and that the prophet's labour, like his prayer, is the appointed channel and not the source. Clarke's rule stands over the scene: do your own work fervently, and beg God to do his.
35:
The restoration comes by degrees: 'he returned, and walked in the house to and fro; and went up, and stretched himself upon him: and the child sneezed seven times, and the child opened his eyes.' John Gill pictures the prophet walking 'in deep thought and meditation, and, no doubt, in fervent ejaculations for the wished-for blessing,' and Charles Ellicott reads the pacing as 'an index of intense excitement,' Elisha 'earnestly expecting the fulfilment of his prayer' (he waves aside à Lapide's odd notion that the prophet walked to work up bodily heat). John Wesley adds that Elisha 'changeth his postures for his own necessary refreshment,' and that the repeated action teaches us 'not to be discouraged in our prayers, if we be not speedily answered.'

The seven sneezes draw special comment. Adam Clarke takes them medically — 'that is, it sneezed abundantly' — explaining that as 'the nervous influence began to act on the muscular system,' contractions and 'sternutation or sneezing would be a natural consequence,' the more so as the fatal disorder lay 'in the head'; he quotes Pliny that 'sneezing relieves disorders of the head.' Ellicott notes the verb occurs only here and denotes 'a faint rather than a loud sneeze,' the 'repeated sneezing a sign of restored respiration' (comparing the young man of Nain, Luke 7:15).

A quiet debate attends the gradualness. Ellicott reports Keil's view that 'whereas Elijah raised the widow's son at once (1 Kings 17:17), his successor only restored the Shunammite's son by degrees,' betokening 'an inferiority on the part of Elisha' — but Ellicott counters that 'the narrative in Kings is plainly abridged,' so the contrast may be illusory. Wesley draws the pastoral point instead: 'although miracles were for the most part done in an instant, yet sometimes they were done by degrees,' and 'the work begun in the former verse is here perfected.' The witnesses agree that the slow, prayerful, step-by-step recovery of the child is itself an encouragement to persevering prayer, and a sign that the God who gives life does so in his own measure and time.
36:
The prophet gives the child back: 'he called Gehazi, and said, Call this Shunammite... And he said, Take up thy son.' John Gill marks only that she was summoned 'up to the chamber to him,' but Charles Ellicott lifts the moment into the Gospel: 'Take up thy son' answers exactly to the way 'our Lord "delivered to his mother" the young man whom He raised from death by His word' at Nain (Luke 7:15). The prophet does not display the miracle or claim the boy; he restores him at once to the arms from which death had taken him.

Keil and Delitzsch supply the tender sequel — that after the boy was raised, 'Elisha had his mother called and gave her back her son, for which she fell at his feet with thanksgiving.' The living child is the answer to the mother's clinging faith and to the prophet's shut-in prayer; the same Gehazi who had carried the useless staff is now sent to carry the joyful summons.

The witnesses feel the quiet triumph of the scene without straining after it. Where at v. 16 the mother had cried 'do not lie unto thine handmaid,' the promise is now doubly kept — not only was the son given, but he is given a second time, back from the dead. The chamber built in kindness has become the room where a mother receives her boy alive; and the plain command, 'Take up thy son,' carries all the weight of restored joy. The commentators also note how the miracle ends as quietly as it began, without a word of self-congratulation from the prophet: he neither parades the wonder before the household nor lingers over it, but simply reunites the boy with the mother who had believed, so that the joy is hers and the glory God's. In it the commentators see a shadow of the greater Prophet who would speak the same mercy over another widow's son, and who holds the keys of death: the God of Elisha is the God who kills and makes alive, and who gives back what he takes, to the wonder and thanksgiving of those who trust him.
37:
The mother's response is wordless worship: 'she went in, and fell at his feet, and bowed herself to the ground, and took up her son, and went out.' John Gill reads her posture as 'reverence of him, and with the most profound gratitude and thankfulness for the mercy received,' and Charles Ellicott agrees she 'bowed herself to the ground in deep veneration for the prophet of Jehovah.' She who had clasped his feet in the anguish of v. 27 now falls at them in the fullness of joy; the gesture of grief has become the gesture of thanksgiving.

Adam Clarke invites us to feel what the narrative only implies: 'Few can enter into the feelings of this noble woman. What suspense must she have felt during the time that the prophet was employed in the slow process' of raising her child — a process 'slow in its own nature, and exceedingly exhausting to the prophet himself.' The bare verbs of the verse cover a mother's unspeakable relief and the costly labour of the man who prayed her son back to life. Keil and Delitzsch note simply that she 'fell at his feet with thanksgiving' before taking up her boy.

The witnesses see the whole pericope come to rest in this silent act. There is no recorded speech — no cry of joy, no rehearsal of praise — only a woman prostrate at the prophet's feet and then rising to gather her living son into her arms and carry him out. Her faith at v. 21, which had laid the dead child on the prophet's bed in hope of resurrection, is here vindicated and crowned; and her worship, offered first at the prophet's feet, rises past him to the God whose servant he is. The commentators observe, finally, that her worship is silent where her grief had been vocal: she who had reasoned and pleaded now has nothing to say, for a gratitude too deep for speech can only bow, and the living weight of her son in her arms is answer enough to every question she had brought. The commentators leave her there, bowed and then blessed, as a lasting picture of the gratitude that answers grace: she takes up her son, and goes.
38:
A new scene opens in famine among the prophetic community: 'Elisha came again to Gilgal: and there was a dearth in the land; and the sons of the prophets were sitting before him: and he said unto his servant, Set on the great pot, and seethe pottage.' John Gill recalls that Gilgal held a school of the prophets, which Elisha visited 'to instruct, encourage, and strengthen them, and to confirm his office by miracles.' Charles Ellicott reads 'came again' as marking 'a new narrative' not in strict chronological sequence, and pictures the sons of the prophets 'sitting before him as disciples before a master, probably in a common hall which served for lecture, work, and dining-room' (cf. ch. 6:1; Ezek. 8:1; Acts 22:3).

Matthew Henry frames the whole passage around Elisha's fatherly care. Here is the prophet 'in his element, among the sons of the prophets, teaching them, and, as a father, providing for them.' The dearth, he notes, was sent 'for the wickedness of those that dwelt therein' and lasted seven years (ch. 8:1); yet though there was 'a famine of bread,' there was 'not of hearing the word of God,' for the disciples still sat before their master (an echo of the coming judgment threatened in Amos 8:11). Henry adds that the sons of the prophets 'should be examples of temperance,' content with a plain 'mess of pottage' of herbs, since 'this great prophet entertained himself and his guests no better.'

The witnesses thus set the two food-miracles that follow within a double frame: physical scarcity and spiritual plenty. Adam Clarke notes the fittingness of the moment — that in 'a time of dearth' the prophet 'was led to put forth the power he had from God to make a plentiful provision.' The great pot is set on not for feasting but for survival, and Elisha, the teacher of the word, shows himself also the provider of bread, caring for the bodies of his students while he feeds their souls. The scene prepares us to see, in the poisoned pottage and the multiplied loaves, a God who keeps his servants alive in evil times.
39:
An honest mistake poisons the meal: 'one went out into the field to gather herbs, and found a wild vine, and gathered thereof wild gourds his lap full... for they knew them not.' John Gill explains that in the dearth the gardens 'afforded none,' so a disciple went to gather what common herbs he could. The identity of the deadly fruit is a genuine botanical dispute. Adam Clarke and the Vulgate call it 'coloquintida,' the 'bitter apple,' about the size of a large orange, whose 'seeds and pulp are intensely bitter and violently purgative,' ranking 'among vegetable poisons.' Keil and Delitzsch, following Celsius and Gesenius, prefer 'wild cucumbers,' a bitter fruit that bursts with a loud noise when ripe and touched, though they record that the ancient versions rendered the word 'colocynths.'
