Charles Ellicott weighs both, noting that the 'wild gourds' or cucumbers are 'oval in shape, taste bitter,' and take their Hebrew name from their habit of bursting when touched; they were 'mistaken for edible gourds, a favourite food of the people' (Num. 11:5), while the Vulgate's 'colocynth' is a related plant with large orange-like fruits 'which are very bitter and cause colic.' The witnesses agree on the essential fact even where they differ on the species: a hungry gatherer, unable to tell the wholesome from the harmful, filled his lap with a bitter, purgative fruit and shredded it unwittingly into the common pot.

Matthew Henry turns the accident to reflection. The sons of the prophets, he observes wryly, 'were better skilled in divinity than in natural philosophy, and read their Bibles more than their herbals.' And he draws the deeper lesson: 'if any of the fruits of the earth be hurtful, we must look upon it as an effect of the curse — thorns and thistles shall it bring forth unto thee (Gen. 3:18) — for the original blessing made all good.' The witnesses see in the tainted pot a small parable of a fallen world, where even the food gathered in good faith may carry death, and where the servants of God stand in need of a power greater than their own knowledge to make their provision safe.
40:
The danger is discovered at the first taste: 'as they were eating of the pottage, that they cried out, and said, O thou man of God, there is death in the pot. And they could not eat thereof.' John Gill sets the plain scene — the pottage poured out into dishes for the sons of the prophets — and Keil and Delitzsch explain that 'the extremely bitter flavour of the fruit so alarmed the pupils of the prophets that they cried out, Death in the pot, and therefore thought the fruit was poison'; and the fear was not groundless, for 'if eaten in any large quantity, colocynths might really produce death.' Charles Ellicott agrees that 'the bitter taste, and perhaps incipient effect of the pottage, made them think of poison.'

Matthew Henry draws the meditation for which the verse is remembered. 'The table often becomes a snare, and that which should be for our welfare proves a trap' — a good reason 'why we should not feed ourselves without fear.' Even in receiving 'the supports and comforts of life,' he urges, 'we must keep up an expectation of death and a fear of sin.' And he notes the wise mercy in our very senses: 'nature has given man the sense of tasting, not only that wholesome food may be pleasant, but that that which is unwholesome may be discovered before it comes to the stomach; the mouth tries meat by tasting it' (Job 12:11).

The witnesses see in the cry 'there is death in the pot' both a real peril and a fitting emblem. The disciples had gathered and cooked in good faith, and still death lay hidden in their food; discernment, given by God, caught it in time. The scene leaves the sons of the prophets helpless before their own dinner — they 'could not eat thereof' — and so sets the stage for the prophet's remedy in the next verse. The commentators press, too, that the very cry names Elisha rightly — 'O thou man of God' — for in their extremity the disciples turn instinctively to the one through whom God has so often acted, bringing their peril to the prophet as the widow and the Shunammite had brought theirs. What human knowledge could not prevent, and human appetite would not risk, only Elisha, and the God who works through him, can undo.
41:
The prophet's cure is simple and swift: 'Then bring meal. And he cast it into the pot... Pour out for the people, that they may eat. And there was no harm in the pot.' John Gill notes only that he cast it in and stirred it, but the meaning of the meal opens a real debate about nature and miracle. Adam Clarke grants that meal 'might, in some measure, correct the strong acrid and purgative quality; yet it was only a miracle which could make a lapful of this fruit shred into pottage salutary.' Keil and Delitzsch put it strongly: the meal 'could not take the injurious qualities entirely away,' and so 'was only the earthly substratum for the working of the Spirit, which proceeded from Elisha, and made the noxious food perfectly wholesome'; they reject the attempt of the naturalist to 'get rid of the miracle' by supposing Elisha added some other remedy.

Charles Ellicott lays the options side by side. Keil holds the meal 'only the material basis for the spiritual activity'; Thenius supposes plainly that 'the meal softened the bitterness and obviated the drastic effect'; but Ellicott judges Reuss 'to be right' — that 'by mistake a poisonous (not merely a bitter) plant had been put into the pot, and the prophet neutralises the poison by means of an antidote whose natural properties could never have had that effect.' On this reading the meal is a sign, not a cause; and Ellicott notes that it 'corresponds to the salt' Elisha had earlier used to heal the spring at Jericho (ch. 2:21).

The witnesses divide, then, between a naturalistic reading (Thenius) and the miraculous one (Keil, Clarke, Reuss, Ellicott), but the weight of the testimony falls decisively on the miracle. As with the salt cast into the deadly water, the meal is the humble, ordinary token through which a divine power works; the wholesome grain, staple of human life, becomes 'the earthly substratum' for the Spirit's healing. The lesson the commentators draw is of a piece with the whole chapter: God delights to join his saving power to plain and common means — a flask of oil, a handful of meal, a prophet's prayer — so that the glory is seen to be his, and the pot of death is turned, at his word, into a pot of life.
42:
A second food-miracle begins with a pious gift: 'there came a man from Baalshalisha, and brought the man of God bread of the firstfruits, twenty loaves of barley, and full ears of corn in the husk... Give unto the people, that they may eat.' Keil and Delitzsch locate Baal-Shalisha in the country west of Gilgal and describe the offering — 'twenty barley loaves and roasted ears of corn' — which Elisha at once orders to be given to the hundred pupils of the prophets. Charles Ellicott places it some fifteen Roman miles north of Lydda, and explains the theology of the gift: the 'bread of the firstfruits' belonged by law to the priests and Levites (Num. 18:13; Deut. 18:4), and 'full ears of corn' render the word for fresh or roasted grain (Lev. 2:14).

Why the firstfruits came to a prophet rather than to Jerusalem the witnesses explain from the divided kingdom. John Wesley notes that these were 'the priests' due' (Num. 18:12), 'but were usually brought by the pious Israelites to the Lord's prophets, because they were not permitted to carry them to Jerusalem'; Adam Clarke calls the bread 'an offering to the prophet, as the firstfruits themselves were an offering to God.' In the northern kingdom, cut off from the temple, the devout brought their sacred dues to God's living representatives, and so, as Matthew Henry observes, helped to maintain the prophetic schools; Ellicott adds that such presents 'appear to have been usual in ordinary times,' but this one came opportunely 'in a time of dearth.'

The witnesses feel the verse straining toward the wonder that follows, and toward the Gospel. One pious man, in a famine, brings his firstfruits to the man of God, who will not keep them for himself but commands, 'Give unto the people, that they may eat' — the very words, Ellicott notes, that anticipate our Lord's charge to feed the multitude (Matt. 14:16). The small loaves laid before the prophet become the raw material of a miracle of increase; and the generosity of the giver, offering his best in a hungry season, is answered by the greater generosity of God, who is about to make twenty loaves feed a hundred men.
43:
The gift seems absurdly small for the need, and the servant says so: 'his servitor said, What, should I set this before an hundred men? He said again, Give the people, that they may eat: for thus saith the LORD, They shall eat, and shall leave thereof.' John Gill takes the servant to be 'very probably Gehazi,' and Charles Ellicott renders his objection, 'How am I to set this before a hundred men?' — the natural arithmetic of unbelief. Against it stands not the prophet's confidence but the word of the Lord: Ellicott notes the Hebrew of the promise is an abrupt, exclamatory 'eating and leaving!', the clipped speech of 'hurried and vehement utterance.'

Matthew Henry catches the contrast of doubt and faith. The servant 'thought that to set so little meat before so many men was but to tantalize them, and shame his master for making so great an invitation to such short commons; but he, in God's name, pronounced it a full meal for them, and so it proved.' The prophet does not argue the mathematics; he simply repeats the command and grounds it in a 'thus saith the LORD.' The multiplication will not be his own doing but God's, and God has spoken.

Adam Clarke lifts the scene to its true author and its Gospel parallel. 'It was God, not the prophet, who fed one hundred men with these twenty loaves'; 'this is something like our Lord's feeding the multitude,' and indeed, Clarke adds, Elisha 'might be more aptly considered a type of our Lord than most of the other persons in the Scriptures who have been thus honored' (cf. Matt. 14:16–17). The commentators feel, too, the boldness of the prophet's position: he pledges a full meal and something over on nothing but a divine word, exposing himself to open shame should the word fail; faith of this order does not calculate the odds, but obeys and waits. The witnesses see the servant's incredulity not as a flaw to be scorned but as the foil that makes the miracle plain: where human reckoning sees only a shameful shortfall, the word of the Lord promises enough and to spare, and stakes the prophet's honour on God's power to provide.
44:
The chapter ends on the quiet fulfilment of the promise: 'So he set it before them, and they did eat, and left thereof, according to the word of the LORD.' John Gill notes plainly that he set the twenty barley loaves and the ears of corn before the hundred, and the deed matched the word. But whether a true increase took place is the closing debate of the passage. Charles Ellicott records that 'Bähr denies any miraculous increase of the food,' making the wonder consist only in the fact that 'the hundred men were satisfied with the little they received, and even had some to spare'; and 'Thenius thinks that the provisions were not inconsiderable for a hundred men,' the emphasis lying 'rather on Elisha's absolute confidence in God than on His wonder-working powers.'

Ellicott firmly resists this thinning-down of the miracle: such a reading 'is certainly opposed to the sacred writer's intention.' He sides with Keil, who 'rightly calls attention to the fact that Elisha does not perform, but only predicts, this miracle' — the increase is God's act, announced by the prophet, so that the glory is unmistakably the Lord's. The threefold stress of the narrative, 'according to the word of the LORD,' will not allow the event to be reduced to a lucky sufficiency; the food multiplied because God had said it would.

Matthew Henry presses the same point with pastoral warmth: 'they did eat, and left thereof, not because their stomachs failed them, but because the bread increased in the eating.' God has promised his church that 'he will abundantly bless her provision, and satisfy her poor with bread' (Ps. 132:15); 'whom he feeds, he fills; and what he blesses comes to much.' All the witnesses acknowledge that 'Christ's feeding his hearers was a miracle far beyond this' (cf. Matt. 14:20), yet both alike teach 'that those who wait upon God in the way of duty may hope to be both protected and supplied by a particular care of divine Providence.' So the chapter of Elisha's mercies closes as it ran — with a God who takes the little that is offered in faith, multiplies it by his word, and leaves his people fed and with something to spare.
